roster.name: Jordan Diallo
roster.role: Player-character protagonist of 'First Bell.' Jordan is the audience surrogate navigating the social ecosystem of a new school in real time. Her defining choice — whether to stay quiet and blend in with the popular crowd, or step in when Reyna becomes a target — is the emotional spine of the episode, forcing the player to weigh belonging against integrity on day one.

roster.name: Marcus Webb
roster.role: Marcus is the gravitational center of the episode's 'easy belonging' path - the social gatekeeper whose approval is the tempting, cheap shortcut to feeling like you've already won on day one. He's not a villain; he's a kid performing a role he half-inherited and is quietly scared of losing, which makes his cruelty toward Reyna reflexive and social rather than personal - and makes him capable, in small moments, of being caught off guard by someone with the nerve to just... not play along.

roster.name: Reyna Castillo
roster.role: Reyna is the emotional stakes of 'First Bell' made human — the quiet test of whether Jordan will choose easy belonging or real integrity. She's not a symbol of victimhood; she's a specific, funny, guarded kid who has learned to expect very little from new people and is quietly, carefully hopeful that Jordan might be different.

roster.name: Devon Park
roster.role: Devon is the low-stakes, unexpected friendship of the episode — proof that not every relationship at a new school has to be a performance or a test. He gives Jordan (and the player) a version of belonging that doesn't cost anything, which makes the contrast with Marcus's crowd sharper: Devon never asks Jordan to be anyone but herself, and that ease is quietly radical. He also models a version of self-protection through invisibility that mirrors what Reyna does more visibly — giving the player a second, gentler lens on the same fear.

roster.name: Ms. Sofia Alvarez
roster.role: The well-meaning adult authority whose distraction — not malice — creates the vacuum where the episode's central choice has to happen. Her lateness in noticing Reyna's public humiliation isn't a plot contrivance; it's who she is under real classroom conditions, and it puts the moral weight of the moment squarely on Jordan rather than letting an adult solve it.
